quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Potential energy function"?

options.text: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
U(x) such that F = -dU/dx; the negative derivative of potential energy gives the force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Potential energy function" is defined as: U(x) such that F = -dU/dx; the negative derivative of potential energy gives the force. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gravitational PE (near Earth)"?

options.text: ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant
a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes
U = mgh; the energy stored when an object is raised height h above a reference level

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gravitational PE (near Earth)" is defined as: U = mgh; the energy stored when an object is raised height h above a reference level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Elastic PE"?

options.text: U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces

quiz_questions.explanation: "Elastic PE" is defined as: U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equilibrium points"?

options.text: Locations where F = -dU/dx = 0; stable if U is a minimum (restoring force), unstable if U is a maximum
ΔKE = KE_f - KE_i; negative for inelastic collisions; equals -½μv_rel² where μ = m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂) is the reduced mass
A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equilibrium points" is defined as: Locations where F = -dU/dx = 0; stable if U is a minimum (restoring force), unstable if U is a maximum. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy, which best defines "Conservative force"?

options.text: A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum
Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r
E_mech = KE + PE; the sum of kinetic and potential energies of a system

quiz_questions.explanation: In Conservative Forces and Potential Energy, "Conservative force" refers to: A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "U(x) such that F = -dU/dx; the negative derivative of potential energy gives the force"?

options.text: Horsepower
Weight
Potential energy function
System of equations

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Potential energy function".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "U = mgh; the energy stored when an object is raised height h above a reference level"?

options.text: Uniform circular motion
Rotational kinetic energy
Gravitational PE (near Earth)
Transverse vs. longitudinal

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gravitational PE (near Earth)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium"?

options.text: Elastic PE
Recoil
Work-Energy Theorem
Impulse approximation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Elastic PE".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Locations where F = -dU/dx = 0; stable if U is a minimum (restoring force), unstable if U is a maximum"?

options.text: Kepler's Third Law
Equilibrium points
Superposition
Transverse vs. longitudinal

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equilibrium points".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Conservative Forces and Potential Energy, which term matches: "A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force"?

options.text: Instantaneous power
Conservative force
Hooke's Law
System of equations

quiz_questions.explanation: In Conservative Forces and Potential Energy, this refers to "Conservative force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Conservative Forces and Potential Energy is accurate?

options.text: Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservative Forces and Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.

The centripetal force is not a new type of force—it is the net radial component of real forces (tension, gravity, normal force, friction) that provides the inward acceleration needed to maintain circular motion.
The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservative Forces and Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservative Forces and Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.
Multiple-force problems require careful free-body analysis at specific positions.
Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservative Forces and Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).
The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
Both approximate SHM for small oscillations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Conservative Forces and Potential Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: Automotive airbag design increasing collision time to reduce peak force on occupants while the same impulse (momentum change) occurs
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region
Spacecraft trajectory planning using Newton's Second Law with thrust force, gravitational force, and changing mass to compute acceleration profiles
Molecular chemistry modeling interatomic bonds as potential energy curves (Lennard-Jones potential) to predict bond length and bond energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: Pole vaulting biomechanics analyzing the conversion of kinetic energy → elastic PE in the pole → gravitational PE at peak height
Interplanetary mission design computing escape velocity from Earth and transfer orbits (Hohmann transfers) to reach Mars or other planets
Roller coaster engineering ensuring the car speed at the top of a loop exceeds √(gr) so riders maintain contact with their seats
Civil engineering analyzing dam stability using gravitational potential energy and identifying stable vs. unstable equilibrium configurations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Conservative Forces and Potential Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Pole vaulting biomechanics analyzing the conversion of kinetic energy → elastic PE in the pole → gravitational PE at peak height
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy?

options.text: Molecular chemistry modeling interatomic bonds as potential energy curves (Lennard-Jones potential) to predict bond length and bond energy
Sports equipment design (golf clubs, tennis rackets) optimizing mass ratios and coefficients of restitution for maximum energy transfer to the ball
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Conservative Forces and Potential Energy in practice?

options.text: Grandfather clock design using the precise period T = 2π√(L/g) of a pendulum to regulate timekeeping mechanisms
Civil engineering analyzing dam stability using gravitational potential energy and identifying stable vs. unstable equilibrium configurations
GPS satellite corrections accounting for the variation of g with altitude to maintain precise timing and positioning
Analyzing car crash data using kinematic equations to calculate braking distances and deceleration rates for traffic safety engineering

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Conservative Forces and Potential Energy.
